scenario: Ridge Wholesaler runs a Camunda 8.8 order-to-delivery process based on a BPMN model with Collect Money, Fetch Items, and Ship Parcel service tasks. 22000 orders per day flow through this XML-backed model, and a new engineer needs to understand how Zeebe drives execution between elements.
question: How does Zeebe advance execution between BPMN elements in this order-to-delivery process?

options.text: Zeebe evaluates the entire BPMN XML on each tick and re-runs every service task whose worker has not yet acknowledged completion to the orchestration cluster
Zeebe maintains a token per process instance and moves it along the sequence flow arrows, stopping at each service task to create a job for a registered worker
Zeebe streams each XML element to a queue and workers compete for service task execution by reading the queue and writing BPMN attributes back for the next element
Zeebe materializes the process as a state chart of tasks and lets workers pick any state to execute, ignoring the sequence flow arrows after the initial deployment

a.text: Incorrect. Zeebe does not re-evaluate the entire BPMN XML on every tick or re-run service tasks; it advances tokens along the sequence flow as each task completes.

b.text: Correct. Process execution is modeled as a token per instance that advances along sequence flow arrows; when the token reaches a service task, Zeebe creates a job and notifies a registered worker, then continues on completion.

c.text: Incorrect. There is no queue model where workers rewrite BPMN attributes for the next element; service task execution uses jobs created by Zeebe at each task.

d.text: Incorrect. Zeebe respects sequence flow arrows to drive tokens; it does not flatten the model into a state chart that ignores the modeled order of execution.

explanation: The BPMN primer describes process execution as tokens running through the model. Zeebe drives the token, stops at each task to create a job for the registered worker, and continues along the outgoing sequence flow on completion.
